For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors

- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

Goal:
prepare Bread Omelette

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Break open 2 eggs in a mixing bowl, then add red chilli powder along with required salt.
- Chop an onion into smaller pieces and add it to the whisked eggs.
- Heat a pan with butter when it is hot - pour the egg mixture on it. Tilt it so that the egg mixture is even on all the edges.
- Take a bread slice dip in the egg then flip over and place it. Repeat this for the other egg too. So, the dipped side should come on top. Drizzle oil or butter.
- Once it gets cooked on one side and flip over to other side and press it with the ladle. Cook for 2 mins then flip over and serve.

Goal:
prepare Cappuccino

- Fill a cup with whole milk and heat it in the microwave for 3-4 minutes. Stir the heated milk with a milk frother to make the cappuccino frothier.
- Take another empty cup and put 2 teaspoons of sugar and coffee in the cup. Add about a half tablespoon of water and slowly add more until the ingredients are just wet so that you can beat the mixture.
- Add more water if needed, about 1/2 teaspoon at a time, whipping it vigorously with the spoon.
- Once the mixture in nicely thickened, smooth, and almost shiny, your cappuccino mixture is ready
- Pour the heated milk in the golden-brown paste and stir it well until all ingredients are uniformly mixed. Heat the cappuccino again in microwave for 30 seconds and it is ready to drink.

Goal:
prepare Chopped Chickpea Salad

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Preheat the oven to 425 degrees F.
- Rinse the chickpeas, then place the chickpeas on a towel and lightly towel dry them so they aren't wet from rinsing. You can remove any loose skins at this time too.
- Place the chickpeas on a baking sheet and drizzle with the olive oil. Toss to coat, then sprinkle with the paprika, garlic powder, oregano, salt and pepper. Stir the chickpeas and shake the pan a few times to distribute the seasoning. Put the tray in the oven and roast the chickpeas for 15 minutes.
- Take apart the head of lettuce. Wash the lettuce leaves and squeeze to dry.
- Stir the chickpeas and roast for 10 to 15 more minutes
- Tear the lettuce and place it in a large bowl.
- Wash the tomatoes and then cut them in half.
- Add the tomatoes to the bowl
- Chop the cucumber into small pieces and add it to the bowl.
- Drain the olives, tear them in half and add them to the bowl.
- Crumble the blue cheese into the bowl.
- Stir the ingredients in the bowl together to mix.
- Remove the chickpeas from the oven and discard any burnt chickpeas.
- Mix the salad and the chickpeas together in a bowl to serve.

prompt:
You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare Mushroom Risotto

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Put dried mushrooms into a large bowl.
- Finely chop the onions.
- Pour boiling water over the dried mushrooms, stir and soak.
- Measure out the pre-chopped garlic.
- Wash and chop the fresh chestnut and shiitake mushrooms.
- Drain the dried mushrooms into a pan, keeping the liquid.
- Crumble vegetable stock cube into the mushroom liquid, then squeeze the mushrooms gently with a fork to remove any liquid.
- Heat 3 tbsp olive oil in a shallow saucepan or deep frying pan over a medium flame. Add the pre-chopped garlic and the finely chopped onion into the pan, then fry for about 5 mins until soft, stirring regularly.
- Measure out the risotto rice.
- Stir in chopped chestnut mushrooms, chopped shiitake mushrooms, and the dried mushrooms
- Season with salt and pepper and continue to cook, stirring regularly, for 8 mins until the fresh mushrooms have softened.
- Tip risotto rice into the pan and cook for 1 min. Pour over a glass of white wine and let it bubble to nothing so the alcohol evaporates.
- Keep the pan over a medium heat and pour in a quarter of the mushroom stock. Simmer the rice, stirring often, until the rice has absorbed all the liquid.
- Add about the same amount of stock again and continue to simmer and stir - it should start to become creamy, plump and tender. By the time the final quarter of stock is added, the rice should be almost cooked.
- Grate the cheese.
- Continue stirring and add the rest of the stock.
- When the rice is cooked take the pan off the heat, add butter and scatter over grated parmesan or Grana Padano cheese and tear and scatter a handful of parsley leaves. Chop the rest of the parsley and save for later.
- Give the risotto a final stir, add more parsley and taste.

Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

prompt:
You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare Banana Bread Chocolate Chip Cookies

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Measure the butter into a microwavable container, and heat it in the microwave. Stir.
- In a large bowl, combine the melted butter and sugar. Mix to combine.
- Place the banana into a microwavable container, and heat it in the microwave. Stir.
- Add the banana to the melted butter and sugar. Mix to combine.
- Separate an egg yolk and add.
- Add vanilla extract. Mix well.
- Measure and add the flour, salt, baking soda, and cinnamon. Stir until combined and no streaks of flour remain.
- Last, fold in the chocolate chips. Set aside to let the flour hydrate in the dough.
- Turn on the oven to preheat.
- Line a cookie sheet with parchment paper.
- Use a cookie scoop or a spoon to scoop out about 1 inch dough balls. Place 2 inches apart on the prepared cookie sheet
- Put in the oven and bake for 10 to 11 minutes or until the edges are set and the cookies have puffed up, but the middles are still underbaked.
- Remove the cookies from the oven

Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:

- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

prompt:
You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare Southwestern Salad

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Preheat the oven to 400 degrees.
- Wash and peel the sweet potatoes and chop into bite-sized pieces. Put the sweet potatoes in a bowl and add the olive oil, cumin, and chili powder. Mix well and pour the mixture onto a baking tray. Roast for 10 minutes.
- Pulse all the dressing ingredients in a food processor until mostly smooth.
- Check the sweet potatoes and roast for another 10-15 minutes.
- Wash and then chop and tear the lettuce into a bowl.
- Add the sweetcorn and beans to the bowl. Chop the rest of the avocado and add.
- Remove the sweet potatoes from the oven.
- Stir the salad and add the dressing. Stir again.
- Add the sweet potatoes.

The action stream has 410 actions. The approximate midpoint is action index 204.
Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

Goal:
prepare Scrambled Eggs

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Crack the eggs into a medium bowl. Whisk until smooth and combined, with no streaks of egg white remaining.
- Spray olive oil in a small nonstick skillet. Bring to medium heat.
- Add chopped red onions and finger chillies to a pan and saute until soft
- Pour in the eggs, and let them cook for a few seconds without stirring. Pull a rubber spatula across the bottom of the pan to form large, soft curds of scrambled eggs.
- Continue cooking over medium-low heat, folding and stirring the eggs every few seconds. Scrape the spatula along the bottom and sides of the pan often to form more curds and to prevent any part of the eggs from drying out
- Remove the pan from the heat when the eggs are mostly set, but a little liquid egg remains. Season to taste with salt and pepper.
- Add cottage cheese to plate and season to taste with salt, pepper, oregano and olive oil.

prompt:
You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare Salmon Teriyaki Skewers with Vegetables

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Preheat the oven to 180 degrees C/gas mark 4.
- Place salmon teriyaki skewers on a baking sheet. Roast for 11 to 15 minutes, until just cooked. Set aside until cool enough to handle.
- Pre-heat air fryer to 180 degrees C
- Place broccoli in air fryer and roast for 15 minutes. Season with oil, salt, pepper, oregano, and garlic granules half-way through.
- Add pre-made Chickpea, Spinach & Quinoa salad to plate.
- Add broccoli and salmon skewers to plate ontop of salad. Serve and enjoy!

The action stream has 422 actions. The approximate midpoint is action index 210.
Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare Tuna Pasta Salad

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Cook the pasta according to the package directions. Drain and rinse in warm water, then transfer to a large bowl. Season with salt and pepper and let cool.
- Add the tuna, coriander, beans, spinach, bell peppers, carrots, celery, onion, cherry tomatoes, cucumbers, and spinach. At this point, you can cover the bowl and refrigerate it until you're ready to serve, up to 6 hours.
- Pour the dressing over the top of the salad and toss everything together to coat. Season with salt and pepper to taste. Give the salad a taste - if you think it could use a splash more oil or vinegar, then go for it! Serve the pasta salad immediately, or let it sit at room temperature for up to 2 hours.

Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

Goal:
prepare Fish Cakes and Vegetables

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Preheat the oven to 180 degrees C

- Boil water for couscous (170 ml)
- Add couscous and boiling water to pot (no flame needed), leave until water has been absorbed
- Slice onion, chili pepper, and bell pepper and place to side
- Add oil to a nonstick pan. Bring to medium heat.
- Once warm, add garlic, onions, red peppers and chilis to pan. Cook until browned
- Add broccoli and green beans to pan and stir
- Season pan with salt, pepper, and sesame oil
- Serve fishcakes on top of couscous with side of vegetables

Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Goal:
prepare Spaghetti Bolognese

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Heat the oil in a frying pan over a medium-high heat. Add the onion and cook for 5 minutes, stirring often, until the onion softens.
- Add the garlic and chilli peppers and cook for a further minute.
- Add the minced beef and cook for 5-6 minutes, until browned. Stir often, breaking up any large chunks of meat with a spatula.
- Add the carrot, celery, bell peppers, and mushrooms and cook for a further 2-3 minutes.
- Add the tomato pasta sauce, oregano, tinned tomatoes, salt, and pepper. Stir and bring to the boil. Turn down the heat and simmer for 15 -20 minutes until thickened.
- Meanwhile, boil a large pan of salted water. Add the spaghetti and cook for 10-12 minutes until al dente.
- Drain the spaghetti and divide it between bowls.

You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare Crispy Cucumber Salad

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Cut the cucumber lengthwise down the middle then into 2.4cm/1 inch pieces
- Salt 30 minutes - Put them in a bowl then toss with salt. Leave for 30 minutes. The salt will draw out water from the cucumbers which dilutes flavour when you eat them. It also seasons the cucumbers all the way though.
- Drain and discard the cucumber water from the bowl (too salty to re-use for another purpose). Use a colander if you want to be thorough else just drain it out of the bowl, using your hand to hold the cucumbers in.
- Whisk the soy sauce and sesame oil in a bowl until it combines properly. You'll know because it becomes murky and thicker, rather than clear and split.
- Add 2 teaspoons of chilli crisp - or more if you dare! You want to get a mound of the crispy chilli - it will be well soaked with the red chilli oil that colours this salad. You can always add more after you toss. Taste and add, taste and add!
- Finally grate and add ginger and garlic to bowl. Toss well then pile up high into a shallow bowl and serve!

You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare Hibiscus Drink

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Add the Hibiscus (Zobo) flowers and chopped ginger into a pot a fill with water
- Cover the pot and bring to a boil for about 30 minutes, occasionally stirring.
- Strain the liquid to extract hibiscus leaves and ginger bits
- Add sugar, give it a good stir and leave to cool.

The action stream has 545 actions. The approximate midpoint is action index 272.
Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

prompt:
You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare Hibiscus Drink

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Add the Hibiscus (Zobo) flowers and chopped ginger into a pot a fill with water
- Cover the pot and bring to a boil for about 30 minutes, occasionally stirring.
- Strain the liquid to extract hibiscus leaves and ginger bits
- Add sugar, give it a good stir and leave to cool.

The action stream has 967 actions. The approximate midpoint is action index 483.
Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

prompt:
You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare Mangsho Bhuna

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Heat oil in a pan and throw in some cinnamon sticks, cardamon, cloves, black peppercorn.
- Add half of the sliced onion and fry until the onion begin to brown. Set aside the other half for later use.
- Add water to the mix along with turmeric and red chili powder and stir.
- Then add in the garlic and ginger paste, ghee, yoghurt and stir.
- Now add the meat to the mix and stir to mix evenly.
- Cover and cook until the meat is cooked through and a bit tender (50 - 55mins), occasionally adding water and stirring to prevent burning and sticking to the pan
- Fry the second half of the onions until browned. Once cooked, add to the rest of the mixture.
- Once the meat is cooked and tender, add the fried onion, cumin, garam masala, ghee and chili and cook through until the meat is tender.

Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

Goal:
prepare Mango Lassi

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Peel and slice mangoes.
- Add mangoes, ice cubes, cold water, honey, coconut milk, and yogurt to blender.
- Blend on high until smooth, approx. 2 - 3 min.
- Slice and peel remaining mango, add to the blender and blend to combine, approx. 30 secs-1 min. Enjoy.

The action stream has 1505 actions. The approximate midpoint is action index 752.
Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.

You are creating one controlled counterfactual mistake for online procedural mistake detection.

Goal:
prepare French Fries and Beef Skewers

High-level recipe steps, for reference only:
- Cut the beef into cubes of your choosing
- Slice the onion, tomato and peppers into preferred size
- Marinate beef in ginger and garlic paste
- Thread the beef and sliced vegetables (onion, tomato and scotch bonnet chilli) onto skewers and arrange them on a baking tray
- Drizzle skewers with oil
- Cook in the oven for 15 minutes or more based on your oven settings, turn them over halfway through.
- Peel the potatoes and cut them onto strips
- Heat the oil in a pan, season it with salt, and add the potatoes in batches
- Once fried, transfer to a plate and season with peri-peri seasoning

Choose exactly one critical action from the candidate actions near this midpoint.

For this task, a critical action means a committed recipe-state change: an action where an ingredient, food item, tool, heat source, cooking vessel, or container is used in a way that directly changes the recipe outcome. Changing this action should be hard to recover from, not something the person can simply undo in the next moment.

Good critical targets usually look like:
- adding, pouring, sprinkling, mixing, stirring, draining, discarding, transferring, or combining ingredients
- putting food or liquid into a specific cooking vessel, container, appliance, oven, pan, pot, fridge, or freezer
- applying heat, choosing cooking temperature or time, starting a cooking/blending/frothing process after ingredients are committed
- using a tool that directly transforms the food, such as cutting, blending, grinding, straining, or frothing

Noise/support actions are not valid targets. Leave these unchanged:
- opening or closing doors, cupboards, drawers, packets, lids, fridges, or appliance doors
- picking up, putting down, holding, moving, checking, looking at, or arranging objects before they are used
- plugging, unplugging, switching power on/off, pressing generic power buttons, or moving cables
- cleaning, wiping, tidying, drinking water, using a phone, walking, waiting, or repositioning the body/camera
- selecting or picking up an ingredient without adding it to the recipe yet

Example: replacing "pick up a jar of spices" with "pick up sugar" is not a good mistake, because the person could notice before adding it. Replacing "add the spices into the pan" with "add sugar into the pan" is a good mistake, because the wrong ingredient has been committed to the recipe.
